crabgrass_preemergent.trigger: Apply before crabgrass germination; use soil temperature and local history rather than a calendar alone.
crabgrass_preemergent.soil_temperature: Around 55F at typical germination depth for several days
crabgrass_preemergent.follow_up: Split applications can extend residual where pressure is long or early applications are used.
cautions: Do not miss the pre-germination window for purely pre-emergent products.

goosegrass_preemergent.trigger: Goosegrass commonly germinates later than crabgrass and favors compacted/high-traffic sites.
goosegrass_preemergent.soil_temperature: Around 60F and later spring pressure windows
goosegrass_preemergent.primary_window: Late spring residual coverage is important
goosegrass_preemergent.follow_up: Use split or extended residual programs where goosegrass pressure persists.
cautions: Correct compaction and thin turf or herbicide programs will underperform.

poa_annua_preemergent.trigger: Target fall germination before Poa annua emerges.
poa_annua_preemergent.soil_temperature: Often increases as soil temperatures decline toward about 70F
poa_annua_preemergent.follow_up: Residual duration matters because germination can occur over an extended period.
cautions: Check turf species, overseeding plans, and label restrictions.

poa_annua_seedhead_suppression.trigger: Begin before visible seedhead emergence based on local growing degree/day models or historical timing.
poa_annua_seedhead_suppression.primary_window: Late winter to early spring depending on region
poa_annua_seedhead_suppression.follow_up: Sequential programs may be needed depending on product and pressure.
cautions: Programs can stress weak Poa annua; adjust around weather and turf health.

white_grub_preventive.trigger: Preventive products should be in place before or near egg hatch and early larval activity.
white_grub_preventive.primary_window: Late spring to early summer for many preventive programs; refine by species and region.
white_grub_preventive.follow_up: Water-in requirements differ by product and target zone.
cautions: Do not treat calendar-only without history or scouting where pressure is unknown.

white_grub_curative.trigger: Curative control is most effective on small, active larvae before severe root pruning.
white_grub_curative.primary_window: Late summer to early fall when larvae are present and feeding near the rootzone
white_grub_curative.follow_up: Confirm larvae with soil sampling before treatment.
cautions: Large mature grubs and drought-stressed turf are harder to recover.

brown_patch_preventive.trigger: Risk increases with warm nights, high humidity, and long leaf wetness.
brown_patch_preventive.air_temperature: Night temperatures above about 68F increase risk
brown_patch_preventive.primary_window: Begin preventive work when warm humid nights become consistent
brown_patch_preventive.follow_up: Reduce leaf wetness and avoid excessive nitrogen during pressure.
cautions: Do not assume all brown patches are Rhizoctonia; diagnose pattern and conditions.

pythium_blight_preventive.trigger: High risk with hot days, warm nights, near-saturated humidity, and wet turf.
pythium_blight_preventive.air_temperature: Day temperatures above about 90F and night temperatures above about 70F increase risk
pythium_blight_preventive.primary_window: Prevent before forecast high-risk hot, humid, wet periods
pythium_blight_preventive.follow_up: Improve drainage and avoid evening irrigation during risk periods.
cautions: Pythium is oomycete-driven; many standard fungicides do not cover it.

summer_patch_preventive.trigger: Root infection begins before summer symptoms appear.
summer_patch_preventive.soil_temperature: Begin around 65F soil temperature at 2 inch depth
summer_patch_preventive.primary_window: Spring preventive window before visible summer symptoms
summer_patch_preventive.follow_up: Water in products intended to reach root infection zones when label calls for it.
cautions: Waiting for summer symptoms is often too late for best preventive control.

spring_dead_spot_preventive.trigger: Infection and preventive treatment planning occur before spring symptoms are visible.
spring_dead_spot_preventive.primary_window: Fall preventive window on bermudagrass sites with history
spring_dead_spot_preventive.follow_up: Support recovery in spring with cultivation and balanced fertility.
cautions: Spring symptoms reflect earlier infection; do not wait until spring for preventive programs.

localized_dry_spot_response.trigger: Respond when soil probing confirms dry/hydrophobic areas, not just visual wilt.
localized_dry_spot_response.primary_window: During active dry-down periods and before severe thinning
localized_dry_spot_response.follow_up: Use wetting agents preventively where LDS recurs; hand water acute hot spots.
cautions: Do not overwater surrounding healthy areas while chasing isolated hydrophobic spots.

aeration_cool_season.trigger: Time disruptive cultivation when turf can recover quickly.
aeration_cool_season.primary_window: Spring or early fall, with fall often safer for cool-season recovery
aeration_cool_season.follow_up: Coordinate with overseeding, topdressing, and pre-emergent restrictions.
cautions: Avoid highly disruptive cultivation during peak heat or severe stress unless emergency drainage/oxygen relief is needed.

aeration_warm_season.trigger: Time cultivation during active warm-season growth.
aeration_warm_season.primary_window: Late spring through summer during active growth
aeration_warm_season.follow_up: Avoid aggressive cultivation too close to dormancy.
cautions: Recovery slows as warm-season turf approaches dormancy.
